content.text: Sequential decision-making under uncertainty is one of the hardest problems in AI. Reinforcement learning (RL) solves it by having an agent discover good behavior through interaction with an environment and feedback in the form of reward signals, rather than from labeled training examples. RL is the engine behind LLM alignment techniques such as RLHF, AlphaGo's superhuman game play, robotic locomotion, and recommendation systems — making it foundational knowledge for anyone building or studying modern AI systems.
from its action space, and receives a scalar
signal that reflects the immediate quality of that action. The agent's objective is to learn a

— a mapping from states to actions — that maximises the total accumulated reward over time. Unlike supervised learning, there is no label telling the agent what the correct action was; it must infer which actions are good from delayed, sparse, and sometimes noisy reward signals.
RL is formally modelled as a

, a mathematical framework in which the environment satisfies the Markov property: the next state depends only on the current state and the chosen action, not on the full history. An MDP is defined by the tuple (S, A, P, R, γ) where S is the state space, A is the action space, P(s'|s,a) is the transition probability function, R(s,a) is the expected reward, and γ ∈ [0,1) is the discount factor that weights future rewards.
— the decision-maker that perceives state and selects actions
— everything outside the agent; it transitions to new states and emits rewards
— a complete description of the environment at a given timestep
— a choice the agent makes from the set A available in state s
— a scalar signal indicating immediate desirability; can be negative (penalty)
— the strategy mapping states to action probabilities that the agent learns
— expected sum of discounted future rewards starting from state s
— expected future reward for taking action a in state s then following π

The most immediate reason RL matters to practitioners working with large language models is RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. After a base LLM is pre-trained and then supervised-fine-tuned (SFT), it still produces responses that may be unhelpful, harmful, or dishonest. RLHF closes this gap by training a reward model on human preference comparisons and then using RL (almost always PPO) to shift the LLM's distribution toward higher-scoring outputs. Without RL, ChatGPT-style instruction following and safety alignment would not exist in their current form.

Beyond alignment, RL is the substrate for any system that must make chains of decisions to reach a long-horizon goal. Agentic LLM systems that orchestrate tool use, write and execute code, browse the web, or manage files are naturally modelled as RL problems — the agent selects tool calls (actions), observes results (state updates), and optimises for task completion (reward). Understanding RL concepts lets engineers reason about why agents fail, how to shape reward signals, and which training strategies to apply.

In broader AI, RL has produced breakthroughs in domains where supervised learning cannot easily be applied: game playing (AlphaGo, AlphaStar, OpenAI Five), robotic control, chip floor-planning, energy optimisation in data centres, and drug discovery. The common thread is that the correct answer is too expensive or impossible to label in advance — only outcome feedback is available.
— the agent learns purely from reward signals; human annotation is only needed to define the reward, not to specify every correct action
— optimises over entire trajectories, not just single-step predictions, making it suited for planning, dialogue, and control tasks
— RL agents routinely find solutions that human designers would not have anticipated, such as AlphaGo's novel opening moves
— the agent can keep training as more environment interactions accumulate, improving without requiring additional labeled data
— by encoding the desired objective in the reward function, RL can optimise for human preference, safety constraints, latency, cost, or any measurable outcome
— with a learned environment model, agents can plan ahead using simulated rollouts, dramatically reducing real-world sample requirements
— millions of environment interactions may be needed before a policy converges; this is prohibitive when each step is expensive (e.g., physical robot trials or GPU inference calls)

— if the reward function does not perfectly capture the intended goal, the agent will find ways to maximise the metric while violating the intent (reward hacking); designing good rewards is an ongoing research problem
— RL training can diverge or collapse, especially with policy gradient methods; hyperparameter sensitivity is significantly higher than in supervised learning

— the agent must balance trying new actions (explore) against using its current best strategy (exploit); getting this balance wrong causes slow learning or premature convergence to suboptimal policies
— as the policy changes, the data distribution changes too, making RL an inherently non-stationary optimisation problem that violates the i.i.d. assumption underlying most ML theory
— the agent may take harmful actions while exploring before it has learned which actions to avoid; this is a major concern in robotics and any production system that trains online
At every timestep t, the agent observes state sₜ, samples an action aₜ from its policy π(a|sₜ), and receives a reward rₜ along with a transition to the next state sₜ₊₁. This interaction produces a
τ = (s₀, a₀, r₀, s₁, a₁, r₁, …). The agent's objective is to find the policy that maximises the expected discounted return:

The discount factor γ (typically 0.95–0.999) makes near-term rewards worth more than distant ones and ensures the sum converges for infinite-horizon problems. Setting γ = 1 gives equal weight to all future rewards; γ = 0 makes the agent purely greedy.
Value functions and the Bellman equation
V^π(s) gives the expected return when starting in state s and following policy π thereafter:
Q^π(s,a) extends this to a specific first action:
The Bellman equation expresses V recursively — a state's value equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state:
to update value estimates from incomplete trajectories without waiting for an episode to end. The simplest update rule is the TD(0) update:
The term in brackets is the
δₜ — how much the current estimate differs from the observed one-step return. Q-learning is an off-policy variant that bootstraps from the maximum Q-value:

attrs.latex: Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha\!\left[r_t + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)\right]

content.text: Policy gradient methods directly parameterise the policy and compute gradients of the objective J(θ) with respect to the policy parameters θ. The policy gradient theorem states:
Here Aₜ = Qᵖⁱ(sₜ, aₜ) − V^π(sₜ) is the
— how much better action aₜ is compared to the average action in state sₜ. Using the advantage instead of the raw return significantly reduces gradient variance.

The RL interaction loop connects four main components: the agent (policy and value networks), the environment, an experience buffer that stores transitions, and the optimiser that updates the agent's parameters. The diagram below shows the information flow across one training timestep.

attrs.alt: Reinforcement Learning loop: Agent, Environment, Experience Buffer, Value Function, and Policy Update

content.text: For LLM alignment, the architecture becomes a three-stage pipeline. The first stage produces a supervised fine-tuned (SFT) model from high-quality demonstrations. The second stage trains a reward model (RM) on human preference pairs using the Bradley-Terry ranking loss. The third stage runs PPO to update the LLM policy, guided by the RM score and constrained by a KL divergence penalty against the SFT checkpoint to prevent catastrophic divergence.

attrs.alt: RLHF pipeline: SFT → Reward Model Training → PPO RL Optimization → Aligned LLM

content.text: RL is well-suited to problems where:
where each choice affects future options (multi-step dialogue, game playing, robotic arm control)
available, even if delayed (win/loss at game end, task completion, user engagement metric)
Supervised labels are unavailable or prohibitively expensive
but environment feedback can be generated by simulation or human judgment
, allowing millions of interactions without real-world cost (game engines, physics simulators, language model rollouts)
The desired behavior is difficult to specify explicitly but easier to
RL is not the right tool when:
A large labeled dataset exists — supervised or self-supervised learning will converge faster and more stably
The reward function is poorly defined or proxy metrics diverge from true objectives, making reward hacking likely
Training compute is severely limited — RL typically requires 10–100x more compute than supervised fine-tuning for equivalent task performance
The action space is astronomically large or continuous without structure — consider imitation learning or offline RL from logged data instead

Implementing a basic RL agent involves five steps: defining the environment interface, initialising the value or policy network, collecting experience through rollouts, computing the update target, and applying a gradient step.
library (successor to OpenAI Gym) is the standard environment interface. Any environment exposes

returning (observation, reward, terminated, truncated, info). For LLM RL, the environment is typically the LLM itself: the state is the conversation history, the action is a generated token or response, and the reward comes from a reward model.
Step 2 — Initialise the Q-table or neural network

For small discrete state spaces, a Q-table (numpy array) suffices. For complex observations (images, text, continuous vectors), use a neural network as a function approximator. Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and actor-critic architectures are the standard approaches for neural function approximation.

Run the current policy for N timesteps or M episodes, storing each transition (s, a, r, s', done) in a replay buffer. Off-policy algorithms like DQN sample randomly from this buffer, breaking temporal correlation. On-policy algorithms like PPO discard the buffer after each update and collect fresh data.

For value-based methods, compute the Bellman target r + γ max Q(s'). For actor-critic, compute the advantage A = r + γV(s') − V(s) using the critic network's value estimates. Generalised Advantage Estimation (GAE) with parameter λ provides a bias-variance trade-off.

Apply gradient ascent on J(θ) for policy networks, and gradient descent on the Bellman error (TD error squared) for value networks. PPO adds a clipping constraint to prevent large policy updates that destabilise training.

import numpy as np

# Frozen-lake style grid: 4 states, 2 actions (0=left, 1=right)
# Terminal state is 3; reward +1 on reaching it, 0 elsewhere
n_states = 4
n_actions = 2

def next_state(s, a):
    if s == 3:
        return s, 1.0   # terminal
    if a == 1:          # move right
        return min(s + 1, 3), (1.0 if s + 1 == 3 else 0.0)
    return max(s - 1, 0), 0.0  # move left

# Q-table: rows = states, cols = actions
Q = np.zeros((n_states, n_actions))

alpha = 0.1   # learning rate
gamma = 0.99  # discount factor
epsilon = 0.2 # exploration rate
episodes = 5000

for ep in range(episodes):
    state = 0
    for _ in range(50):  # max steps per episode
        # Epsilon-greedy action selection
        if np.random.rand() < epsilon:
            action = np.random.randint(n_actions)
        else:
            action = np.argmax(Q[state])

        next_s, reward = next_state(state, action)

        # Q-learning (off-policy TD update)
        td_target = reward + gamma * np.max(Q[next_s])
        td_error  = td_target - Q[state, action]
        Q[state, action] += alpha * td_error

        state = next_s
        if state == 3:
            break

print("Learned Q-table:")
print(np.round(Q, 3))
print(f"Optimal action in state 0: {'right' if np.argmax(Q[0]) == 1 else 'left'}")

The following two examples demonstrate exploration strategies — the core challenge that every RL algorithm must address. The first uses UCB1, a theoretically grounded bandit algorithm. The second shows the REINFORCE policy gradient on a one-armed bandit.

Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1) selects the arm that maximises a score combining the empirical mean and an exploration bonus that shrinks as confidence grows. Unlike epsilon-greedy, UCB1 has a theoretical regret bound of O(log T).

import numpy as np

# Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1) bandit — exploration strategy
# that is more principled than epsilon-greedy
np.random.seed(42)

n_arms = 5
true_means = [0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 0.4, 0.6]   # unknown to agent

counts    = np.zeros(n_arms)
estimates = np.zeros(n_arms)
n_rounds  = 2000

for t in range(1, n_rounds + 1):
    if t <= n_arms:
        arm = t - 1   # pull each arm once at start
    else:
        # UCB score: estimated mean + exploration bonus
        ucb_scores = estimates + np.sqrt(2 * np.log(t) / counts)
        arm = np.argmax(ucb_scores)

    reward = np.random.normal(true_means[arm], 0.3)
    counts[arm] += 1
    estimates[arm] += (reward - estimates[arm]) / counts[arm]

best_arm = np.argmax(estimates)
print(f"Estimated means: {np.round(estimates, 3)}")
print(f"True means:      {true_means}")
print(f"Best arm found: {best_arm} (true best: {np.argmax(true_means)})")
print(f"Pulls per arm: {counts.astype(int)}")
REINFORCE directly optimises the policy parameters using the policy gradient theorem. A running average reward serves as a baseline, reducing gradient variance without introducing bias.

import numpy as np

# REINFORCE (vanilla policy gradient) on a simple 1-step bandit
# Action space: {0, 1, 2}, preference for action 1 (reward = 1)
np.random.seed(7)

def softmax(x):
    e = np.exp(x - x.max())
    return e / e.sum()

def reward_fn(action):
    return 1.0 if action == 1 else 0.0

n_actions = 3
theta = np.zeros(n_actions)   # policy parameters
alpha = 0.05                  # step size
baseline = 0.0                # running average reward (variance reduction)

for episode in range(3000):
    probs  = softmax(theta)
    action = np.random.choice(n_actions, p=probs)
    r      = reward_fn(action)

    # Update baseline
    baseline += 0.01 * (r - baseline)

    # Policy gradient: ∇J = (r - b) * ∇log π(a|θ)
    # Gradient of log softmax: δ(a==i) - π(i)
    grad = -probs.copy()
    grad[action] += 1.0
    theta += alpha * (r - baseline) * grad

final_probs = softmax(theta)
print(f"Policy probabilities: {np.round(final_probs, 3)}")
print(f"Action 1 probability: {final_probs[1]:.3f} (should be near 1.0)")

The training of GPT-4, Claude, and similar models includes an RLHF stage that runs at the scale of billions of parameter models, tens of thousands of human preference labels, and thousands of GPU hours of PPO training. The reward model is typically a classifier head attached to an LLM backbone, trained on pairwise preference data using the Bradley-Terry loss:

where y_w is the preferred response and y_l is the rejected response. After training, the reward model is frozen and used to score LLM rollouts during PPO. The PPO objective for LLM alignment adds a KL penalty to keep the policy close to the SFT baseline:

The coefficient β (typically 0.01–0.1) controls how much the policy is allowed to deviate from the SFT checkpoint. Too small and the policy reward-hacks; too large and the RL step has no effect. In practice, β is tuned per-model and monitored throughout training using KL tracking dashboards.
The code below sketches the minimal reward model training setup using Hugging Face TRL, the standard library for LLM RL workflows:

# Minimal RLHF reward model training sketch (using transformers + trl)
# Run: pip install transformers trl datasets

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
from trl import RewardTrainer, RewardConfig
from datasets import Dataset

# Simulate preference dataset: pairs of (chosen, rejected) responses
examples = [
    {
        "input_ids_chosen":   [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],   # tokenised chosen response
        "attention_mask_chosen": [1, 1, 1, 1, 1],
        "input_ids_rejected": [1, 6, 7, 8, 9],   # tokenised rejected response
        "attention_mask_rejected": [1, 1, 1, 1, 1],
    }
] * 20  # toy dataset, replicated

dataset = Dataset.from_list(examples)

# Load base model to fine-tune as reward model
model_name = "distilbert-base-uncased"
model     = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(model_name, num_labels=1)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

# RewardTrainer wraps the Bradley-Terry preference loss
config = RewardConfig(
    output_dir="./reward_model",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    remove_unused_columns=False,
)

trainer = RewardTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=config,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)

# trainer.train()   # uncomment to actually train
print("Reward model trainer configured.")
print("Loss: -log σ(r_chosen - r_rejected) per Bradley-Terry model")

At production scale, PPO typically runs for 2,000–10,000 update steps over ~100,000 prompts sampled from a prompt distribution representative of real user queries. Multiple reward models covering different quality dimensions (helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty) may be combined linearly.

— the agent finds proxy behaviours that maximise the reward metric without achieving the true goal. Example: an LLM trained to maximise a length-sensitive reward starts adding unnecessary padding. Mitigation: audit reward signals, use diverse reward models, include human eval alongside automated metrics.

— if reward is only given at episode end (e.g., chess win/loss), the agent may never discover a winning trajectory. Mitigation: use reward shaping, potential-based reward functions, or curriculum learning that increases difficulty gradually.
Ignoring the KL penalty in RLHF
— without constraining deviation from the SFT model, the policy collapses to repetitive or degenerate high-reward outputs within a few hundred PPO steps. Always monitor KL divergence during training.
— storing only recent transitions causes the agent to forget past experiences and overfit to recent behaviour, creating catastrophic forgetting cycles.
Learning rate too high for policy gradient

— large policy updates push the policy far into low-probability regions, producing bad rollouts that permanently destabilise training. Use small learning rates (1e-5 to 3e-4) and monitor average episode reward.
— mixing stale rollout data into PPO updates causes an importance-weight mismatch that violates PPO's trust region guarantee. Always collect fresh data with the current policy.
Start with a strong baseline policy
— initialise RL from supervised fine-tuning (imitation learning) rather than random weights; this drastically reduces sample requirements and training instability
— subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation of advantages within each minibatch; this stabilises gradients across batches with varying reward scales
— track episode return, episode length, entropy, KL divergence, value loss, policy loss, and gradient norms at minimum; RL bugs often appear as subtle metric anomalies before catastrophic failure
— write unit tests that assert the reward function returns expected values for known good and bad trajectories before connecting it to the training loop
— add a small entropy bonus (H[π] = −Σ π log π) to the objective to prevent premature policy collapse; adjust the coefficient as training progresses
Evaluate on held-out prompts with human review
— reward model scores can overfit to the training distribution; periodic human evaluation is the only reliable quality signal for LLM RL
— apply gradient clipping (max_norm=1.0) to prevent exploding gradients from large policy updates or rare high-reward episodes
RL training is fundamentally compute-bound and parallelism is the primary scaling lever. Two strategies dominate:

— run N copies of the environment simultaneously (e.g., using gymnasium's VectorEnv or Ray RLlib), collecting N transitions per step. This gives an O(N) speedup in data throughput with no algorithmic changes.
— separate actors (environment workers) from learners (GPU parameter servers). IMPALA and Ape-X follow this pattern, enabling thousands of CPU actors to feed one or more GPU learners asynchronously.
For RLHF at LLM scale, the performance bottleneck shifts to the LLM inference cost during rollout generation. Optimisations include:
— PagedAttention and continuous batching increase generation throughput by 10–20x over naive implementations
— bf16 for policy and value networks, with fp32 gradient accumulation, halves memory use
— recompute activations during backward pass to fit larger models in GPU memory at the cost of ~30% extra compute
— shard optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs to train models larger than a single GPU's memory

Sample complexity scales poorly with state space dimensionality. Q-learning with a tabular representation has complexity O(|S||A|/ε²) for ε-optimal policies. Neural approximators partially sidestep this but introduce approximation error that can compound through Bellman backups — known as
— the standard environment interface and collection (CartPole, MuJoCo, Atari). Install:
— production-quality implementations of PPO, SAC, DQN, A2C with consistent API. Install:
— distributed RL at scale; supports distributed PPO, IMPALA, APPO, and custom environments on multi-GPU or multi-node clusters
— Hugging Face library for RLHF, DPO, GRPO, and reward model training on top of transformers. Install:
— educational implementations of VPG, TRPO, PPO, DDPG, TD3, SAC with detailed documentation
— single-file, research-grade implementations of key RL algorithms; excellent for understanding implementation details without abstraction overhead
— essential for tracking RL training metrics; RL experiments are notoriously hard to debug without rich telemetry

# PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) — conceptual implementation
# showing the key clipped surrogate objective

import numpy as np

def ppo_loss(old_log_prob, new_log_prob, advantage, clip_epsilon=0.2):
    """
    Compute the PPO clipped surrogate objective for one action.

    old_log_prob: log π_old(a|s) from data collection
    new_log_prob: log π_theta(a|s) from current policy
    advantage:    A = Q(s,a) - V(s), how much better than average
    clip_epsilon: max allowed policy change ratio (typical: 0.1-0.3)
    """
    ratio = np.exp(new_log_prob - old_log_prob)   # π_new / π_old

    # Clipped objective: prevent large policy updates
    unclipped = ratio * advantage
    clipped   = np.clip(ratio, 1 - clip_epsilon, 1 + clip_epsilon) * advantage

    # Take the minimum (pessimistic bound)
    return min(unclipped, clipped)

# Simulated rollout data
old_log_prob = np.log(0.3)   # action had 30% probability under old policy
advantage    = 1.5            # action was better than average

# Under updated policy, ratio increases:
for new_prob in [0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.7]:
    loss = ppo_loss(old_log_prob, np.log(new_prob), advantage)
    ratio = new_prob / 0.3
    print(f"π_new={new_prob:.1f}  ratio={ratio:.2f}  PPO loss={loss:.4f}")

# With negative advantage (bad action), PPO also clips the downside:
print("\nNegative advantage (penalizing bad action):")
for new_prob in [0.3, 0.2, 0.1]:
    loss = ppo_loss(old_log_prob, np.log(new_prob), -1.0)
    ratio = new_prob / 0.3
    print(f"π_new={new_prob:.1f}  ratio={ratio:.2f}  PPO loss={loss:.4f}")
Several training paradigms address sequential decision-making or preference alignment. The table below compares RL against common alternatives across key dimensions:
Discrete action spaces, small to medium environments
LLM RLHF, continuous control, on-policy settings
When expert data exists; no reward needed
When online interaction is unsafe or costly
LLM alignment; bypasses RL training loop entirely
Task adaptation when labels are available
LLM reasoning; simpler critic than PPO
RL systems face a distinct set of security and reliability risks compared to supervised models, arising from the training loop's dependence on environment feedback:

— if an adversary can influence the human preference data used to train the reward model, they can steer the aligned LLM toward harmful outputs. Mitigations include data provenance tracking, annotator agreement checks, and multiple independent reward models.

— during PPO training, a single bad batch of high-variance gradients can push the policy to a degenerate mode (e.g., outputting empty responses) from which recovery is difficult. Monitor entropy and KL divergence and checkpoint frequently.

— a policy that learned to game an automated reward model may behave unpredictably on real user requests that differ from the training distribution. Always include out-of-distribution evaluation before deployment.

— agents exploring during training may take harmful actions in real environments. Constrained RL (adding safety constraints to the objective via Lagrangian methods) and conservative initialisation from SFT reduce this risk.
— RL training is stochastic and sensitive to random seeds, hardware, and floating-point rounding. Save all seeds, hyperparameters, and environment versions for every training run.
learning to play a game by playing it

. There is no answer key and no teacher labelling each move as correct. The agent tries an action, observes how the game responds (state change + reward), and slowly builds intuition about which moves tend to lead to winning. The reward signal is the only guide.
For RLHF specifically, the mental model is:
the reward model is a learned taste

. A human cannot score every response the LLM generates, so a smaller model is trained to approximate human judgment. PPO then steers the LLM toward responses this taste model prefers, while the KL penalty keeps the LLM from drifting so far that it loses its general capabilities.
RL trains an agent through trial-and-error interaction with an environment, optimising cumulative discounted reward rather than per-step prediction accuracy
The MDP framework (state, action, reward, transition, discount) provides the mathematical foundation; the Bellman equation enables recursive value estimation
Q-learning and policy gradient methods are the two foundational algorithm families; PPO dominates practical use because of its stability and on-policy correctness
RLHF applies PPO to align LLMs using a reward model trained on human preference pairs; the KL penalty against the SFT model is critical to prevent collapse
DPO bypasses the RL loop entirely by reformulating preference alignment as a supervised classification problem — simpler and more stable than PPO for many alignment tasks
Reward design is the most failure-prone part of any RL system; poorly specified rewards reliably produce reward hacking, regardless of how good the algorithm is
Sample efficiency is RL's biggest weakness; start from a supervised baseline, use parallel environments, and prefer offline RL when real interactions are expensive
Monitor KL divergence, policy entropy, value loss, and episode return throughout training — RL bugs manifest as metric anomalies well before catastrophic failure
